science_subject.phenomenon: How we picture electric and magnetic fields
science_subject.key_measurement: MMS mission multipoint sampling of Earth's magnetosphere (NASA, 2014)
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific physics visualization: smooth magnetic field lines around Earth magnetosphere context with MMS spacecraft scale reference, clinical grade, illustrative field topology, no monopole artifacts

sources.citation: NASA MMS mission — Magnetospheric Multiscale observatories at Naval Research Laboratory (2014)
Griffiths D.J., Introduction to Electrodynamics, 4th ed. (Pearson, 2013)

beats.speech_text: You cannot see a magnetic field — but you can map it. Field lines are our honest sketch of which way a compass needle would point, smoothly, everywhere in space.

Electric and magnetic fields assign a force direction to every point. We draw lines tangent to that direction — denser where the field is stronger, never crossing because direction would be ambiguous.
Spacecraft like NASA's Magnetospheric Multiscale mission sample the real magnetosphere at multiple points — turning invisible fields into measured vectors we can plot.
Watch the payoff: closed loops threading from pole to pole around Earth — illustrative topology, not a photograph, but faithful to how measured fields are drawn.
beats.visualization_prompt: Earth magnetosphere with smooth magnetic field line loops and MMS spacecraft context, physics @2 plate fidelity, clinical color

beats.speech_text: Field lines are more than decoration — they predict plasma motion, auroras, and how satellites must navigate space weather.
